ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indonesian green chili sambal (sambal cabe ijo). FOODON_03311737 (chili pepper sauce) is the closest parent category covering chili-based condiment pastes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli sauces and pastes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for sambal cabe ijo in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to sambal cabe ijo or Indonesian green chili condiment paste specifically.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sambal cabe ijo as a condiment is approximately one tablespoon (15 g), used as a side sauce or dipping condiment alongside rice dishes, grilled meats, or fried foods.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chili condiment paste) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central raw green chili pepper (FDC ID 170108) and USDA SR Legacy shallot, garlic, and vegetable oil components; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian chili-based condiments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal cabe ijo as a prepared product. Nutrient values are estimated from a typical recipe composition: approximately 60% green chili peppers, 15% shallots, 10% garlic, 5% tomato, 5% cooking oil, 5% other aromatics and salt. USDA FoodData Central raw green chili (FDC ID 170108), shallot (FDC ID 170501), garlic (FDC ID 169230), and vegetable oil (FDC ID 172336) were used as component references. FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Southeast Asian chili pastes was used for cross-validation. Values represent a per-100g estimate of the prepared sambal paste.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and pepper-containing foods — analogous estimate based on raw green chili pepper and chili sauce GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal cabe ijo in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the GI of raw green chili peppers and chili-based condiment sauces, which are consistently reported in the low GI range (GI < 20) due to their very low carbohydrate content, high capsaicin content, and high dietary fibre relative to available carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (15 × 10.2 / 100) × 0.15 ≈ 0.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact. This estimate is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili-containing condiments.

hypertension.guidance: Sambal cabe ijo contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1 tablespoon (15 g, ~63 mg sodium) and account for cumulative sodium from other meal components to remain within the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load per serving (~0.2)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal cabe ijo has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a negligible glycemic load per tablespoon serving, making it a suitable condiment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; capsaicin in green chilies may additionally support insulin sensitivity, though portion control of the overall meal remains essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.8 g per 100 g) and presence of beneficial phytochemicals from green chili
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal cabe ijo is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and the green chili base provides vitamin C and capsaicin with potential anti-inflammatory properties; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake from this condiment as part of overall dietary sodium management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Cabe Ijo (also written Sambal Cabai Ijo or Sambal Cabai Hijau) is a traditional Indonesian green chili condiment paste made primarily from fresh green chilies (cabe ijo), shallots, garlic, tomato, salt, and cooking oil, sometimes enhanced with lime leaves, galangal, or lemongrass for fragrance. The paste is typically prepared by grinding or blending the raw ingredients and then briefly sautéing the mixture in oil until fragrant, producing a vibrant green, coarsely textured condiment with a fresh, grassy heat and a slightly tangy, savory depth. Unlike red sambal varieties, sambal cabe ijo has a milder, more herbaceous heat profile and a characteristic bright green color. It is widely consumed across Indonesia, particularly in Padang (Minangkabau) cuisine and Javanese cooking, where it accompanies rice, grilled fish, fried chicken, tempeh, and tofu. Nutritionally, sambal cabe ijo is low in calories (~98 kcal per 100 g), low in saturated fat, and provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (~72 mg per 100 g) and dietary fibre (~2.8 g per 100 g). Its glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) warrants portion awareness for those with hypertension. The product sold in jars (220 g) represents a commercially prepared homemade-style version available in Singapore through Indonesian food vendors.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for sambal cabe ijo as a prepared product. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level USDA FoodData Central data for green chili peppers, shallots, garlic, and cooking oil, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Southeast Asian chili pastes. The GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili-based condiments. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level estimation methodology is well-supported and the food's macronutrient profile is consistent across multiple reference sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a typical sambal cabe ijo recipe composition using USDA FoodData Central component data (green chili FDC 170108, shallot FDC 170501, garlic FDC 169230, vegetable oil FDC 172336) and FAO/INFOODS SEA cross-validation. No single approved-source entry exists for this specific prepared product. 2. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this food type. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based condiment containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as green chili-based condiments contain no vitamin D; confirmed zero by formulation. 5. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated oils are used in traditional sambal preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. 6. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based condiment; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for chili-based condiments; no direct GI measurement exists for sambal cabe ijo. 8. The product description states 220 g jar at SGD 10.50 from Smile Martabak - Funan on field survey data; this commercial context is preserved from the input record. 9. Sodium estimate (~420 mg per 100 g) reflects typical salt addition in homemade-style sambal; actual sodium may vary by recipe.
